summary:
The Middle Elamite period, spanning approximately 1500 to 1100 BC, was a phase of significant power and cultural achievement for Elam, the ancient civilization centered in the Khuzestan lowlands and adjacent highland areas of what is now southwestern Iran. During this era, Elam emerged as one of the principal powers of the ancient Near East, engaging in diplomatic and military competition with Babylonia and Assyria. The Middle Elamite kings adopted Akkadian as a language of official correspondence while continuing to develop their own Elamite scribal tradition.

The period is particularly notable for major building activity at Susa, the main lowland capital, and for the construction of [[event:1851|Chogha Zanbil]], a great ziggurat complex built under King Untash-Napirisha around 1250 BC that remains one of the best-preserved ziggurats in the world. Elamite military strength during this period was demonstrated by raids into Babylonia: around 1155 BC Elamite forces sacked Babylon, ending the [[event:1829|Kassite dynasty]] and carrying off important monuments including the famous stele of Hammurabi's code, which was taken to Susa as war trophy. This act both demonstrated Elamite dominance and inadvertently preserved one of antiquity's most important legal documents.

The Middle Elamite period ended around 1100 BC with a period of internal fragmentation and renewed pressure from Mesopotamian powers. Despite its relative obscurity in popular historical awareness, Elam represents one of the longest-lived and most sophisticated civilizations of the ancient Near East, with roots stretching back to the fourth millennium BC.
